Hospital Operations & Patient Analytics

SQL • Healthcare Analytics • Data Exploration • Relational Database

Analyzed a hospital database containing over 75,000 records using SQL to uncover operational, financial, and clinical insights related to patient encounters, insurance coverage, procedure costs, and readmission patterns.

Healthcare organizations generate large volumes of operational data that can be used to improve patient outcomes and financial performance. This project applies SQL-based analytics to identify utilization trends, quantify uncompensated care, evaluate procedure costs, and analyze readmission behavior to support data-driven decision making.

Project Overview

The analysis explores multiple aspects of hospital operations using relational SQL queries across patient, encounter, payer, and procedure datasets.

Records Analyzed

75K+

Database

Hospital

Primary Tool

SQL

30-Day Readmissions

771

Uninsured Encounters

48.7%

Analysis Areas

6

Analysis Areas

  • Encounter volume trends and length of stay
  • Insurance coverage and uncompensated care
  • Procedure cost analysis
  • Patient utilization and readmission patterns
  • Demographic analysis by age and gender
  • Cost-per-encounter by service line

Encounter Volume Analysis

The first analysis examined encounter volume over time. SQL aggregation queries were used to calculate annual encounter totals and classify encounters into ambulatory, outpatient, wellness, urgent care, emergency, and inpatient categories. Percentages were then calculated to understand how the hospital's service mix changed over time.

Insurance Coverage & Cost Analysis

Insurance coverage was analyzed by calculating the proportion of encounters with zero payer reimbursement. The analysis found that nearly half of all encounters received no payer coverage, representing a substantial uncompensated care burden.

Zero Coverage Rate

48.7%

Uncovered Encounters

13,586

Total Encounters

27,891

Additional SQL joins between encounters and payer tables identified average claim costs by insurance provider. Medicaid and uninsured patients generated the highest average claim costs.

Procedure Cost Analysis

Procedure data was analyzed from two perspectives: the most frequently performed procedures and the most expensive procedures based on average cost. This approach highlights both operational volume and financial impact.

The most expensive procedure identified was Admit to ICU, with an average cost nearly four times higher than the second most expensive procedure, Coronary Artery Bypass.

Patient Behavior & Readmission Analysis

Patient utilization patterns were evaluated using quarterly admission trends. SQL window functions were then used to identify each patient's next encounter and determine whether a readmission occurred within 30 days.

The analysis was extended to compare traditional 30-day readmissions with a broader 90-day window, identifying additional patients who would not be captured using the standard healthcare quality metric.

Demographic Readmission Analysis

Readmission records were joined with patient demographic information to evaluate risk across age groups and gender. Patients were grouped into age bands (0–17, 18–40, 41–60, 61–80, and 80+) to identify populations with elevated readmission rates.

Cost Per Encounter by Service Line

The final analysis calculated average billed cost, payer coverage, uncompensated cost, and coverage percentage for each encounter class. This financial breakdown identifies which service lines generate the greatest financial risk for the hospital.

Key Insights

Outpatient-Focused Hospital

Approximately 95.9% of all encounters lasted less than 24 hours, indicating that the hospital primarily delivers outpatient and same-day care.

High Uncompensated Care Burden

Nearly half of all encounters (48.7%) received no payer coverage, highlighting a significant financial challenge associated with uncompensated care.

Readmission Risk

A total of 771 patients returned within 30 days, with a small group of high-utilization patients accounting for a disproportionate share of readmissions.

Procedure Cost Variation

ICU admissions were substantially more expensive than all other procedures, emphasizing the financial impact of critical care services.

Extended Monitoring

Expanding the readmission window from 30 to 90 days identified additional at-risk patients who would not be captured under the standard quality metric.

Financial Performance

Inpatient encounters generated the highest average uncompensated cost per service line, making them the largest contributor to financial risk.
